name: Tonatiuh
description: Tonatiuh, in Aztec mythology, was the sun god of the Aztec empire. He was considered the leader of Tollan and the fifth and current sun. According to the Aztec creation myth, the world had gone through four previous suns, each destroyed by cataclysm. Tonatiuh demanded human sacrifice to sustain him and ensure the sun's daily journey across the sky. Without this nourishment, the sun would fail to rise, plunging the world into eternal darkness. He is often depicted as a fierce warrior, adorned with eagle feathers and holding a shield. His face is typically red, symbolizing the heat and energy of the sun. Tonatiuh's role was crucial for the survival of the Aztec people, as he provided light, warmth, and life-giving energy. He was closely associated with warfare and sacrifice, reflecting the Aztec belief that human blood was necessary to maintain cosmic order. The god's image was prominently featured on the Aztec calendar stone, emphasizing his central importance in their cosmology. Tonatiuh was believed to reside in the heavens during the day and journey through the underworld at night, battling forces of darkness before emerging again at dawn. He embodies the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, and is a powerful symbol of the Aztec worldview.
subtitle: The Aztec Sun God of the Fifth Sun

keyMyths.title: The Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: Tonatiuh became the fifth sun after Nanahuatzin sacrificed himself in a fire. This act brought light to the world, initiating the current era.

keyMyths.title: Daily Journey
keyMyths.description: Tonatiuh traverses the sky each day, requiring human blood to maintain his strength. Without sacrifice, he would fail and the world would end.

keyMyths.title: Underworld Passage
keyMyths.description: At night, Tonatiuh journeys through the underworld, battling forces of darkness. This journey is perilous and requires assistance from other deities.

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún

worshipPractices.name: Human Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: The most significant form of worship was human sacrifice, believed necessary to nourish Tonatiuh and ensure the sun's continued movement. Warriors, especially those captured in battle, were often offered as sacrifices.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, animals, and precious objects were made to Tonatiuh. These offerings were intended to appease the god and ensure his continued favor.

worshipPractices.name: Sun Dances
worshipPractices.description: Elaborate sun dances and ceremonies were performed to honor Tonatiuh and celebrate the sun's power. These ceremonies often involved music, dancing, and feasting.

festivals.name: Toxiuhmolpilia (New Fire Ceremony)
festivals.description: This major festival marked the completion of a full Aztec calendrical cycle. All fires were extinguished, and a new fire was kindled on the chest of a sacrificial victim, symbolizing the renewal of the sun's power and the continuation of the world.

festivals.name: Panquetzaliztli
festivals.description: Although primarily dedicated to Huitzilopochtli, this festival also involved honoring the sun. It included feasts, dances, and the presentation of offerings to both deities.

cultural.modernLegacy: While direct worship of Tonatiuh ceased with the Spanish conquest, his image remains a powerful symbol of Aztec identity and heritage. He is often depicted in art, literature, and popular culture as a representation of the sun's life-giving energy and the strength of the Aztec empire. Representations of Tonatiuh are also found in modern Mexican cultural symbols.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Aztec cosmology, Tonatiuh represents the fifth and current sun, also known as Nahui-Ollin, the Sun of Movement. According to the Aztec creation myth, the world had previously been ruled by four other suns, each destroyed by a different cataclysm. The gods gathered in Teotihuacan to decide who would become the new sun. Nanahuatzin, a humble and diseased god, and Tecuciztecatl, a proud and wealthy god, volunteered. Nanahuatzin bravely leapt into a sacrificial fire and emerged as Tonatiuh, the radiant sun. Tecuciztecatl hesitated and then followed, becoming the moon, but his light was dimmed as punishment for his lack of courage. This myth highlights the importance of sacrifice and humility in the Aztec worldview and explains the origin of the sun and moon.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Tonatiuh's primary myth revolves around his daily journey across the sky. The Aztecs believed that he was a warrior who fought each night to overcome the forces of darkness. He was carried across the sky each day by the souls of fallen warriors and women who died in childbirth, who were considered to be equally courageous. At sunset, he entered the underworld, Mictlan, where he battled the forces of darkness before being reborn each morning. This constant struggle required sustenance, which the Aztecs believed was provided by human sacrifice. The myth of Tonatiuh underscores the Aztec belief in the cyclical nature of time, the importance of sacrifice, and the constant battle between opposing forces.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: The worship of Tonatiuh was central to Aztec religious life. Temples were dedicated to him, and priests conducted elaborate rituals to ensure his continued favor. The most significant aspect of his worship was human sacrifice, believed necessary to provide him with the energy to continue his daily journey. The hearts of sacrificial victims were offered to Tonatiuh, symbolizing the transfer of life force. Captives taken in war were considered the most suitable sacrifices, as their deaths honored both Tonatiuh and the Aztec state. Festivals were held in his honor, featuring music, dance, and the presentation of offerings. The priests played a crucial role in interpreting the will of Tonatiuh and ensuring the proper performance of rituals.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Although the Aztec empire fell to the Spanish in the 16th century, Tonatiuh continues to hold a significant place in Mexican culture and identity. His image is often used to represent the Aztec civilization and its rich history. Depictions of Tonatiuh can be found in art, literature, and popular culture. The most famous representation is the Sun Stone, also known as the Aztec calendar stone, which features Tonatiuh's face at its center. This iconic artifact serves as a powerful symbol of Aztec cosmology and artistic achievement. The legacy of Tonatiuh reminds us of the complex religious beliefs and cultural achievements of the Aztec people.

shortDescription: Tonatiuh was the Aztec sun god and leader of Tollan, the fifth and current sun. He required human sacrifice to sustain his daily journey across the sky.
